questions.question: A theme park sells ride tickets with a two-block price schedule: the first 10 rides cost $4 each and additional rides cost $2 each. A visitor's marginal willingness to pay for rides is P = 12 - Q. Ignoring income effects, how many rides will the visitor buy?
questions.explanation: For the first block, price is $4, and the visitor would buy until 12 - Q = 4, or Q = 8, but the lower second-block price begins only after 10 rides. The 10th ride has willingness to pay $2, equal to the second-block price threshold issue, so the visitor reaches 10 but buys no additional rides because for Q > 10, 12 - Q < 2. A ignores the bundle threshold. C treats the $0 marginal point incorrectly. D buys units with willingness to pay below price.

questions.question: A gym offers a two-part plan: a fixed monthly fee F plus a per-visit price of $5. A student's monthly demand for visits is P = 25 - Q. Marginal cost is $5. What fixed fee extracts all consumer surplus while keeping the student willing to join?
questions.explanation: With per-visit price equal to MC = $5, the student chooses Q = 20. Consumer surplus is 0.5(25 - 5)(20) = 200, so the maximum fixed fee is $200. A halves the surplus incorrectly. C uses only the price difference times half the wrong quantity. D uses Q = 10 instead of Q = 20.

questions.question: A phone company offers a quantity discount: $30 per GB for the first 2 GB and $10 per GB after that. If marginal cost of data falls from $8 to $4, what is the likely effect on the optimal second-block price, holding demand types fixed?

choices.A: The second-block price tends to fall because serving extra usage becomes less costly, making larger bundles more attractive for surplus extraction.
choices.B: The second-block price tends to rise because lower marginal cost requires a higher markup.
choices.C: The second-block price must stay at $10 because second-degree discrimination never responds to cost.
choices.D: The second-block price becomes zero because marginal cost is lower.

questions.explanation: Lower marginal cost makes additional units cheaper to provide, so the seller can profitably encourage more usage through lower marginal prices while extracting surplus through fixed or inframarginal charges. B reverses the cost effect. C falsely treats the menu as cost-independent. D assumes price must fall all the way to zero.

questions.question: A software firm sells Basic and Pro plans. High-demand users value extra features more than low-demand users. If the difference between high-demand and low-demand users' willingness to pay increases, what happens to the firm's incentive to distort the Basic plan downward?

choices.A: It increases because making Basic less attractive helps induce high-demand users to self-select into Pro.
choices.B: It decreases because larger differences make all incentive constraints irrelevant.
choices.C: It disappears because second-degree price discrimination becomes first-degree price discrimination.
choices.D: It causes the firm to price Basic below marginal cost for all users.

questions.explanation: In second-degree price discrimination, menus are designed for self-selection. When high types value quality much more, the firm may make the low-end option less attractive to reduce high types' temptation to choose it. B overstates separation. C confuses self-selection with perfect information. D is not generally implied.

questions.question: A block-pricing graph shows a consumer's downward-sloping demand for electricity and a marginal price that falls after 500 kWh. What graph feature explains why usage jumps at the block threshold for some consumers?

choices.A: The marginal price schedule has a kink, so once the consumer reaches the cheaper block, additional units are compared with a lower marginal price.
choices.B: The demand curve becomes horizontal after 500 kWh.
choices.C: Average cost must rise at exactly 500 kWh.
choices.D: Consumer surplus is impossible under block pricing.

questions.explanation: A declining block tariff creates a kink in the budget/payment schedule. Consumers compare marginal willingness to pay with the lower marginal price after the threshold. B invents a demand change. C adds an unsupported cost claim. D is false.

questions.question: In a two-part tariff diagram, the per-unit price equals MC, and the fixed fee equals the area under demand and above MC up to the chosen quantity. What does the fixed fee represent?

choices.A: Extracted consumer surplus from access to the product.
choices.B: Deadweight loss from pricing above MC.
choices.C: Marginal cost of the last unit.
choices.D: The slope of the budget constraint.

questions.explanation: When the usage price equals MC, the fixed fee can extract the consumer surplus generated by efficient usage. B is wrong because P = MC creates no usage distortion. C confuses fixed and marginal components. D refers to the per-unit price, not the fixed fee.

questions.question: Why does second-degree price discrimination not require the seller to know each buyer's exact type?

choices.A: Because buyers reveal information through their choices among quantity or quality packages.
choices.B: Because all buyers are charged their exact willingness to pay.
choices.C: Because resale is always impossible in competitive markets.
choices.D: Because the seller sets one uniform price for all units.

questions.explanation: Second-degree price discrimination uses self-selection: different types choose different packages. B describes first-degree discrimination. C is neither sufficient nor the defining feature. D describes uniform pricing.

questions.question: A cloud storage company offers 100 GB for $5 per month, 1 TB for $12 per month, and 5 TB for $30 per month. It does not know each user's exact demand, but users choose their preferred plan. Which model best applies?

choices.A: Second-degree price discrimination through a menu of quantity plans.
choices.B: First-degree price discrimination because each user pays their maximum willingness to pay.
choices.C: Third-degree price discrimination because each user is assigned a group by age.
choices.D: Perfect competition because users choose voluntarily.

questions.explanation: The firm offers a menu of quantities and lets users self-select, which is second-degree price discrimination. B would require individualized willingness-to-pay information. C would require observable group pricing. D confuses voluntary choice with competitive pricing.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes second-degree from third-degree price discrimination?

choices.A: Second-degree uses self-selection among menus; third-degree charges different prices to observable groups.
choices.B: Second-degree charges each individual their exact willingness to pay; third-degree uses quantity discounts.
choices.C: Second-degree requires no market power; third-degree requires perfect competition.
choices.D: Second-degree always eliminates deadweight loss; third-degree always increases it.

questions.explanation: Second-degree discrimination is menu-based and induces self-selection. Third-degree discrimination segments buyers into observable groups. B confuses first-, second-, and third-degree discrimination. C is false because price discrimination requires market power. D makes unsupported welfare claims.

questions.question: A two-part tariff charges a per-unit price equal to MC = $4. A representative consumer has demand P = 24 - Q. What fixed fee extracts all consumer surplus, and what is total surplus from trade?
questions.explanation: With per-unit price equal to MC, Q = 20. Total surplus is the triangle under demand above MC: 0.5(24 - 4)(20) = 200. The maximum fixed fee extracts exactly that consumer surplus, so F = 200. B halves the fee. C halves total surplus. D uses an incorrect quantity.
